course_description: This course is an introductory level examination of the basic principles of taxation law in Australia, with particular emphasis on the topics required by professional accounting bodies for accreditation purposes.

requirements.restrictions: Not for students enrolled in LLB(Hons)(#64), LLB(Hons)(#48), LLB, LLB (Grad Entry), LLB(Hons) duals and LLB dual programs
requirements.incompatible: LAWS5144 or LAWS7012 or LAWS7144 or LAWS7344
requirements.assumed_background: Students are assumed to have studied and passed LAWS1100 Business Law (or its equivalent) and to have retained from that course an understanding of the Australian legal system, the ability to apply legal knowledge and legal reasoning in the analysis and resolution of legal problems, and an understanding of the core differences between sole trader businesses, partnerships and companies.

timetable:
Timetables are available on mySI-net.

Additional Timetable Information
Lectures will be recorded and posted to the Learning Resources folder in the course site on Learn.UQ (Blackboard).

Class attendance is encouraged as it is not always possible to record all of the practical examples completed during lectures using the document camera (docucam) system.

Attendance at all tutorials is highly recommended. Only brief summaries of answers to tutorial questions are posted to the course site on Learn.UQ.

Next 2. Aims
course_aims:
Teaching Mission Statement

The mission of the TC Beirne School of Law is to educate students in and about the law. We seek to assist you to think critically about the law and to investigate its structures, principles, policies, and values. This is achieved by engaging with you as fellow investigators in a range of critical and intellectual conversations about the law and its place in society. Our role is thus to guide you in your own intellectual journey in the law and to equip you with the necessary skills that will enable you to continue that journey long after you have left the School.

Course Aims:

To equip students with sufficient knowledge in Australian income tax law to enable them to deal with clients, business persons and legal advisers in an informed and reasoned manner.

To demonstrate the development of legal principles through case law and statutory provisions.

To equip students with the skill and technique to calculate taxable income, tax payable, Fringe Benefits Tax payable and Goods and Services Tax payable or refundable.

To instil an attitude of professionalism and an awareness of ethical standards with regard to:

presentation of written work;

dealings with University staff;

interaction with peers; and

interaction with members of the professions.

To enhance verbal communication skills by encouraging students to participate in discussion and debate of legal issues.

To inspire students to approach the study of law with a keen and inquiring mind, and to maintain life-long interest in legal issues.

learning_outcomes.description: Prove a knowledge of the regulatory framework of the taxation system in Australia.

Demonstrate an understanding of the income tax equation and its elements and be able to apply those elements to the case of a taxpayer. Demonstrate an understanding of the operation of Goods and Services Tax and Fringe Benefits Tax.

Interpret provisions of the relevant Income Tax Assessment Act and other relevant legislation and apply them by preparing a concise written argument sufficient to form the basis of sound advice to a taxpayer and/or by calculating the taxation and financial implications of a particular transaction.
Critically evaluate case law as well as Rulings & Determinations issued by the Commissioner of Taxation.
Solve legal problems/issues relating to assessable income, deductions, capital gains tax, tax payable and tax offsets, Fringe Benefits Tax and the Goods and Services Tax.
Demonstrate the ability to research broad issues in the area of income tax law.
Discuss legal issues raised relating to taxation law in a confident and informed manner.

assessment_summary.category: Exam - Mid Semester Outside Scheduled Class
assessment_summary.title: Mid Semester Exam

assessment_details.title: Tutorial Work and Participation
assessment_details.task_description:
There are ten (10) tutorials for this course. The maximum marks available for each tutorial is one-and-a-half marks. Therefore, if a student meets all of the criteria for seven (7) out of ten (10) tutorials, they have obtained the maximum marks for tutorials. The maximum total marks for tutorials is ten (10).

Attendance at all tutorials is strongly recommended, particularly since brief summaries of model answers are posted to Learn.UQ, not the model answers.

Students who are unable to attend a tutorial may compare their answers to the model answers during the course coordinator's consultation hours.

As the capacity in tutorial rooms is limited, and due to Occupational Health and Safety requirements, room capacity cannot be exceeded. Therefore, students must attend the tutorial they are signed on to. If room capacity is exceeded, a roll will be called which significantly reduces the time available to discuss tutorial answers.

Photography is not permitted during tutorial classes. Recording the tutorial classes is not permitted.

Answers to tutorial questions are discussed during tutorial classes. Therefore, it is important that students prepare sufficiently detailed answers to all tutorial questions so that they are able to add to and/or amend their answers during tutorial classes. Tutorials are not meant as an opportunity to write down answers.

Each tutorial will comprise of more than one tutorial questions (tasks). Answers to all the tutorial questions (tasks) must be completed in writing, typed up, to meet the assessment criteria.

There is no specific font type, font size or spacing requirements that must be used to prepare tutorial answers. But answers should be easy to read.

Students are to retain a copy of their tutorial answers so that they may have this available during tutorial classes.

Submission of tutorial answers: Upload one file, either as a PDF document or as a Word document in each separate folder, created for each separate tutorial on Learn.UQ, using the relevant Link in the relevant teaching week in the Learning Resources folder. The UQ policy for penalties for late submission applies to the late submission of tutorial answers.

Submission:

Tutorial answers must be submitted electronically in PDF format OR Word format via the Turnitin Link in the specific submission folders in each relevant week on the course site on Learn.UQ - See Section 4 of the ECP for due dates and times.

assessment_details.title: Mid Semester Exam
assessment_details.category: Exam - Mid Semester Outside Scheduled Class
assessment_details.format: Multiple-choice, Short answer, Short essay, Practical, Problem solving
assessment_details.task_description:
The Mid Semester Exam will take place as follows:

Date: Wednesday 10 April 2019.

Time: 18:30 to 20:10.

Venues: UQ Union (Heath and Holt rooms).

The Mid Semester Exam will contain multiple choice questions, short theory questions, short essay questions, practical problems, calculation questions and problem solving questions. Some calculation questions will require students to present all calculations, formulas, bases and reasons for answers, while other calculation questions may only require the final answer to be recorded. Students must read the instructions to each question carefully and present their answers in accordance with the requirements of each question to complete the exam in the duration time. Marks are awarded according to the specific question which is asked. The tutorial questions, Mid Semester practice paper, lecture examples and case studies contain examples of the different types of questions which may be included in the Mid Semester Exam.

A Mid Semester Exam practice paper will be released to the course site in Week 5.

The Mid Semester Exam will cover:

Topic 1, 2, 3 and 4.

All the course content covered during Week 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5.

Tutorial 1, 2, 3, 4, 5.

Students are not required to follow the ILAC-approach (Identify the issue – State the law – Apply the law to the facts – Conclude) when answering the questions, but must solve the legal problems in questions by providing appropriate and sufficient explanations in their answers.

Most questions require students to present reasons for answers, bases and formulas, references to the relevant sections of legislation, case law and relevant documents issued by the ATO. Most questions require that workings must be shown.

The Mid Semester Exam is an Open Book exam. Bi-lingual dictionaries are permitted, but no electronic devices and watches are allowed in the exam venues.

Students must bring a University approved calculator to the Mid Semester Exam.

The email enquiry facility for the Mid Semester Exam closes at 16:30 on Monday 8 April 2019.

Additional consultation hours with Dr Voogt are available from 11:00 to 12:30 on Monday 8 April 2019.
assessment_details.criteria_and_marking:
UQ Students: Please access the profile from
Learn.UQ

or
mySI-net

to access marking criteria held in this profile.

assessment_details.title: Final Exam
assessment_details.format: Multiple-choice, Short essay, Practical, Problem solving
assessment_details.task_description:
The Final Exam will cover material from all topics. The questions may include multiple-choice questions. The questions will include discussion questions and calculation questions that are practical and involve problem solving. To meet the assessment criteria, appropriate explanations must be provided that require stating the relevant sections of relevant legislation and/or case law, relevant ATO documents in narrative form. Answers must solve the legal problems posed and show all workings.

A detailed Final Exam Readings and Instructions document will be posted to the Learn.UQ course site in Week 12.

Students are not required to follow the ILAC-approach (Identify the issue - State the law - Apply the law to the facts - Conclude) when answering questions, but must solve the legal problems in questions by providing appropriate explanations for answers, including in calculation questions. Workings must be shown.

The Final Exam is an Open Book exam. Bi-lingual dictionaries are permitted, but no electronic devices and watches are allowed in exam venues.

Students must bring a University approved calculator to the Final Exam .

learning_resources.required_resources:
Principles of Taxation Law (2019). Thomson Reuters. Kerrie Sadiq et al.

As income tax law changes continuously, you should ensure that you obtain the 2019 edition of this textbook.

You are not required to purchase the relevant legislation, or the 'Tax Kits'. It is only the single textbook which is required as learning resource.

As a part of the UQ Law Alumni Association Textbook Programme, a number of copies of the required text for this course have been purchased for the high use section of the Law Library collection. The UQLA is grateful for the generous financial support of alumni and the publishers for making this possible.

Learn.UQ LAWS3101 Course Website (Blackboard).

The LAWS3101 course site on Learn.UQ (Blackboard) contains most of the material required for this course.

Students must check the course site at least twice weekly.

Pay careful attention to the readings for each topic.

The Learning Resources folder contains the lecture recordings and course material, on a week-by-week basis.

Some topics are presented over more than one week, but one set of study notes, case studies and lectures slides will be made available per topic. Refer to the Learning Activities programme in section 4.1 of the course ECP.

The readings for each topic folder will be made available a week in advance of the lecture/s for that topic.

Weekly announcements are posted to the course site. The weekly announcements form an important part of the learning process in LAWS3101.
learning_resources.recommended_resources:
Students may find resources such as the Australian Taxation Office (ATO) website, incorporating the ATO Legal Database helpful.

There is a useful list of internet resources in Principles of Taxation Law at [2.480].

A reference material folder is available for each topic on the course site on Learn.UQ. Each folder contains additional readings, but which is not examinable, incorporating relevant ATO Rulings and other documents relevant to each topic.

The following acts will be referenced during the course:

Income Tax Assessment Act 1997

Income tax Assessment Act 1936

A New Tax System (GST) Act 1999

Fringe Benefits Tax Act 1986.

These are available online:

Australian Legal Information Institute, www.austlii.edu.au

ATO, www.ato.gov.au

Parts of the aforementioned legislation which are examinable will be specifically included in the study notes for the relevant topics.

Australian Taxation Law Cases (2019). Thomson Reuters. Krever.

Students who wish to engage further with the cases referenced in Principles of Taxation Law will find this textbook useful, but this is not a required or prescribed textbook, since the case summaries in Principle of Taxation Law are sufficient.
learning_resources.university_learning_resources:
Access to required and recommended resources, plus past central exam papers, is available at the UQ Library website ( http://www.library.uq.edu.au/lr/LAWS3101 ).

The University offers a range of resources and services to support student learning. Details are available on the myUQ website ( https://my.uq.edu.au/ ).
learning_resources.school_learning_resources:
Law School Citation Style

Students preparing assignments, research papers, dissertations and theses are required to follow the Australian Guide to Legal Citation (4th edition) .

For a short summary of the complete Australian Guide to Legal Citation , see the UQ Library's Referencing Guide to the AGLC .

Law Library

The Law Library's Legal Research Guide site at http://guides.library.uq.edu.au/legal-research-guide provides links to a wide range of Australian and International sites relevant to the study of law.

Students should refer to the UQ Library website generally for information relating to facilities, collections and services available - http://www.library.uq.edu.au .

Course Learn. UQ (Blackboard) Website

Course Learn.UQ (Blackboard) websites contain course announcements; the course profile; the learning guide (where applicable); lecture slides and discussion boards. Students should visit their course Learn.UQ (Blackboard) websites regularly to check for course announcements and to read and contribute to any online activities.

Course Coordinators will maintain and regularly update course Learn.UQ (Blackboard) websites, which can be accessed via the following link – https://learn.uq.edu.au/ . It is your responsibility to ensure that you have access to the websites . Access to Learn.UQ (Blackboard) is automatically generated for those students enrolled through my SI-net approximately 1 week prior to the course commencing. If you do not have access by that time please contact the AskUs Library Help – askus@library.uq.edu.au .

The University of Queensland Library offers computing help and training free to UQ students - https://web.library.uq.edu.au/library-services/it .
learning_resources.other_learning_resources:
The course site on Learn.UQ (Blackboard) will contain all necessary resources and information not included in the prescribed textbook.

Case studies for each Topic are available.

A practice exam will be released prior to the Mid Semester Exam and Final Exam.

The content, additional consultation hours and email enquiry facility cut-off for the Mid Semester Exam is set out in Section 4 and Section 5 of the ECP.

A detailed Final Exam Readings and Instructions document is released detailing the content of the Final Exam, together with special exam consultation hours etc.

learning_activities.topic: Mon Week 1 : Topic 1: The Taxation System in Australia. This topic introduces students to Income Tax, Goods and Services Tax and Fringe Benefits Tax. The topic then focuses on the Income Tax equation and a range of income-based taxes that are payable. The topic concludes with basic principles around tax avoidance and how the tax system in Australia is administered. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 7 Readings/Ref: PoTL (Chapters 1, 3, 15, 19, 22, 23, 24); Course Website (Week 1 - Topic 1)
Fri - Fri 14:00 Tutorial 1 - Online submission (Tutorial Series) : Answers to Tutorial 1 must be uploaded to the course site before Friday 2 pm using the Link in Week 1.

Mon Week 2 : Topic 2: Residence, source and tax accounting. This topic examines the key concepts of residence and source and how these impact income tax payable. It further examines when income is derived and expenses incurred. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 7 Readings/Ref: PoTL (Chapters 4, 16 & 22); Course Website (Week 2 - Topic 2)
Mon - Sun Tutorial 1 : Material from Topic 1 Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 Readings/Ref: PoTL (Readings for Week 1 - Topic 1); Course Website (Week 1 - Topic 1)
Fri - Fri 14:00 Tutorial 2 - Online submission (Tutorial Series) : Answers to Tutorial 2 must be uploaded to the course site before Friday 2 pm using the Link in Week 2.

Mon Week 3 : Topic 3: Income. This topic examines the concept of income from employment, property and carrying on a business. Learning Objectives: 2, 3, 4, 5, 7 Readings/Ref: PoTL (Chapters 5, 6, 8, 9, 10, 21 & 23); Course Website (Week 3 - Topic 3)

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the University's assessment-related procedures can be found on my.UQ. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/services/exams-and-assessment )

Academic Integrity

It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Academic Integrity Tutorial - Support for Students

It may seem simple – don’t cheat and do reference your work. However, there are many issues involved in understanding what is meant by academic integrity and how you could be misled into engaging in an action which may be academic misconduct. The Academic Integrity Tutorial (AIT) will enable you to uphold academic integrity throughout your studies at UQ and beyond. All students are strongly encouraged to complete the module. Some course coordinators may require you to complete the module as part of your assessment.

Plagiarism

The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Student Integrity and Misconduct policy ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.60.04-student-integrity-and-misconduct ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Applications for Extensions

An application for an Extension of Assessment Due Date for medical grounds and/or other exceptional circumstances shall be submitted by lodging the appropriate (online) form with supporting documentation by the date specified in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile. Students will be notified of the outcome of their request via the myRequests section of my.UQ (and an email sent to their student account).

Additional details associated with extension requests, including acceptable and unacceptable reasons, may be found at my.UQ .

Request for Deferral of Exams

A request for deferral of an exam must be submitted by lodging the appropriate (online) form with supporting documentation via mySI-net > my Requests no later than five (5) calendar days after the date of the original exam. Students are encouraged to familiarise themselves with the information on deferring an exam available on my.UQ .

Feedback on Assessment

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the my.UQ web site. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/academic-progress-and-final-results/querying-result )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

Supplementary assessment

A supplementary assessment is designed to give you a second chance at achieving a passing grade for a course, should you receive a final course grade of 3 (or N subject to the Supplementary Assessment Procedures ). It isn't a repeat of an exam, or a specific assignment – it's designed to test the learning outcomes for the entire course, or specific learning outcomes tailored to you.

Supplementary assessment can take any form (e.g. oral, written, examination etc.), and the highest grade you can receive is a 4 (or P). Supplementary assessment isn't available for every course and is only granted in accordance with the Supplementary Assessment Procedures .

Please check the course profile to see if your course is fully or partially exempt from supplementary assessment. If you have any questions about supplementary assessment, or if you're unsure whether you qualify, please contact staff in your school office or faculty office.

Course grade cut-offs

Any change to grade cut-offs will be in accordance with section 4.7.5 of PPL 3.10.02 Assessment – Procedures .

Feedback in this Course

There are several feedback opportunities for students in this course:

Tutorials provide students with small-group feedback about their answers to particular questions and an opportunity to ask questions.

The Mid Semester Exam's detailed model answer will be posted to the course site on Learn.UQ to provide students with feedback about the legal principles which are examined and appropriate exam technique.

In-class quizzes at the end of some lectures will recap on students' understanding of topics.

A large number of case studies with detailed answers are posted to the course site on Learn.UQ to provide valuable learning and feedback opportunities.

Practical problems are discussed during lectures.

Feedback opportunities during consultation hours.

Students are encouraged to email their questions to the course coordinator during the course of the semester. This is a unique feature of the feedback in this course.

School of Law
Assessment Guidelines

Communication with Examiners

Students who have completed an item of assessment should not contact the examiners about that item of assessment prior to the release of the grades or marks for that item of assessment. For queries of an administrative nature, please contact Law School Reception.

After the release of the final grades for the course, feedback may be sought on examination performance in accordance with the School’s policy ( https://law.uq.edu.au/files/7645/TL14-Feedback-on-final-examinations.pdf ).

Deferred Examinations ( mid-semester and end of semester )

Where a student does not attend an examination, the student will in the normal course of events receive zero marks for the examination.

In accordance with Section 6 of PPL 3.10.11 Examinations – Procedures , permission to sit a deferred examination will only be granted in the event of a certified medical condition or other exceptional circumstances which prevented attendance at the examination. Note – except in truly exceptional circumstances, a student who attends whole or part of the original examination will not be eligible for a deferred examination.

Requests for deferral of an examination must be submitted online via mySI-net (myRequests tab) within 5 calendar days following the date of the examination. Supporting documentation must be attached to the request.

Deferred mid-semester examinations will be held in the period nominated by the School for the holding of mid-semester examinations. Deferred end of semester examinations will be held during the University’s deferred examination period.

Students should refer to my.UQ for further information relating to eligibility and how to request a deferred examination.

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses

Students on a placement course – also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice – should refer to the University policy, Placement Courses ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.04-placement-courses ) for detailed information.

Working with Children

Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/1.60.07-working-children ) to find out how to apply for a ‘blue card’.

Students with a Disability

Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Advisor at Student Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.08-alternative-academic-arrangements-students-disability ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.09-arrangements-reasonable-adjustments-examinations-students-disability#Procedures ).

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety

Undergraduate and Postgraduate Students should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/2.30.14-occupational-health-and-safety-laboratory ).

Fitness to Practise

Students enrolled in programs and courses that include a practical placement component (also known as work integrated learning, clinical immersion, clinical placement, clinical practice, externship, fieldwork, industry experience, industry study, internship, practicum, teaching practice, work placement) should refer to the University policy Fitness to Practise ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.30.14-fitness-practise ) for detailed information on issues of competence and on management of concerns relating to patient and public safety.

Other
School of Law
Guidelines

Responsibilities of Students

Students are expected to take responsibility for their own learning and academic development. This includes:

devoting sufficient time and effort to your studies to make and maintain adequate progress,

preparing for lectures and tutorials,

reading prescribed materials,

undertaking such independent study as necessary to reach an understanding of the materials,

completion and submission of assessment items on time,

compliance with School, Faculty, and University rules.

Student Workload

For each 2 unit course students are expected to undertake at least 7 – 8 hours per week of independent study in addition to the time spent in lectures and tutorials.

Student and Staff Communication

If you email a staff member directly, you must include in your email your name, your student number and the name of the course you are enquiring about. Courtesy is always appreciated.

If you telephone a staff member directly and leave a message on their voicemail, you must provide contact details other than a mobile telephone number except where this is absolutely unavoidable.

While the use of telephone and email is acceptable for brief communications with a staff member, you should not expect detailed responses to questions about the law. These questions should be raised on the discussion board, in tutorial sessions or in staff consultation sessions.

Law School Website

Students should refer to the Law School website for more information including enrolment information and important dates; student announcements; news and events; information on Law School prizes and scholarships etc – http://www.law.uq.edu.au .

Previous 5. Assessment

Next Summary
course_introduction:
The overall aim of this introductory course in income tax law is to provide students with comprehensive knowledge of the basic principles of Income Tax, Goods and Services Tax and Fringe Benefits Tax. This course takes a practical approach to income tax law.

The course examines principles of Income Tax law, including an analysis of the concepts of income, assessable income, allowable deductions and capital gains, and the calculation of taxes payable. The course also examines Goods and Services Tax and Fringe Benefits Tax and how these two taxes interact with Income Tax. It is not the objective of this course to study in detail all of the specific and technical provisions in the legislation which govern matters which affects the actual preparation of income tax returns and business activity statements.

Janine McMaster-Kirkwood Prize in Income Tax Law : The student with the best performance in their first attempt at LAWS3101 will be recommended for this prize.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

Changes to the course material, assessments and the tutorial program have been implemented to take into account prior year feedback.

The Mid Semester Exam will include multiple choice questions. The Final Exam may include multiple choice questions. This method of assessment is used by professional accounting bodies.

At the start of the 2018 academic year, the duration of the Mid Semester Exam was extended from 60 minutes to 90 minutes. The weighting of the Mid Semester Exam and Final Exam as components of the final mark for the course was also amended at the start of the 2018 academic year. These changes are retained for the 2019 academic year.

From the 2018 academic year, the taxation of partnerships and the taxation of trusts were added to the course content.

In 2019, tutorial questions will include end-of-chapter questions from the prescribed textbook, Principles of Taxation Law 2019. The submission day and time for tutorials are different to previous years.

The Mid Semester Exam will be written on a weekday evening as scheduled in Learning Activities, available in Section 4 of this ECP.

other_learning_activities_information:
The Function of Lectures

Lectures are a forum where, within an analytical framework offered by the lecturer, students and the lecturer can:

Explore concepts of law relevant to the topic,

Explore how the particular concepts of law work, and relate to other concepts learned,

Identify the central conceptual, intellectual, and doctrinal questions raised by the material under consideration.

In a negative sense, lectures are not for:

Delivering an exclusive and exhaustive account of the material that the student needs to learn,

Reading material that students can read for themselves.

The Function of Tutorials

Tutorials offer students an interactive forum within which to explore in more detail and in greater depth the issues introduced in lectures and to engage with the tutorial staff and other students in exploring those issues. Tutorials also offer students an opportunity to develop their skills in legal reasoning and argument.

As an interactive learning forum, the tutorial is dependent upon all students having prepared the material to be considered and being prepared to engage in debate.

Tutorials are not an opportunity to take down answers, but a forum to discuss answers. Since tutorials provide an inquiry rich forum and feedback opportunity, answers to tutorials are not released to the course site.

course_grading:
Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Fail: Fails to demonstrate most or all of the basic requirements of the course:

0% - 19%

Grade 2, Fail: Demonstrates clear deficiencies in understanding and applying fundamental concepts; communicates information or ideas in ways that are frequently incomplete or confusing and give little attention to the conventions of the discipline:

20% - 47%

Grade 3, Fail: Demonstrates superficial or partial or faulty understanding of the fundamental concepts of the field of study and limited ability to apply these concepts; presents undeveloped or inappropriate or unsupported arguments; communicates information or ideas with lack of clarity and inconsistent adherence to the conventions of the discipline:

48% - 49%

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrates adequate understanding and application of the fundamental concepts of the field of study; develops routine arguments or decisions and provides acceptable justification; communicates information and ideas adequately in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

50% - 64%

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrates substantial understanding of fundamental concepts of the field of study and ability to apply these concepts in a variety of contexts; develops or adapts convincing arguments and provides coherent justification; communicates information and ideas clearly and fluently in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

65% - 74%

Grade 6, Distinction: As for 5, with frequent evidence of originality in defining and analysing issues or problems and in creating solutions; uses a level, style and means of communication appropriate to the discipline and the audience:

75% - 84%

Grade 7, High Distinction: As for 6, with consistent evidence of substantial originality and insight in identifying, generating and communicating competing arguments, perspectives or problem solving approaches; critically evaluates problems, their solutions and implications:

85% +
late_submission:
Where a non-examination item of assessment (eg essays, assignments etc) is not submitted by the due date and time, a penalty will be levied at the rate of 10% of the marks available for the assessment item per day that the assessment is overdue. The date and time of submission will be deemed to be that noted on the electronic submission.

Example: Where a piece of assessment is due at 4pm on Monday, assessment submitted after 4pm Monday but before 4pm on Tuesday will receive a 10% penalty.

Assessment submitted more than 5 days after the due date and time will receive zero marks.

Example: Where a piece of assessment is due at 4pm on the 10th, any work submitted after 4pm on the 15th will receive zero marks.

If the 5th day following the due date is a Saturday or a Sunday, then the date and time of submission will be the time the assessment was electronically submitted.

Example: A piece of assessment is due at 4pm on Monday the 9th. In the normal course of events, to receive any marks, a student must submit their assessment electronically by 4pm on Saturday the 14th (the 5th day following the due date) at the latest.

No extension granted may exceed 14 days.

For full details, students should refer to the Guidelines for Applying for an Extension . Except in exceptional circumstances, extensions for assessment will only be considered in respect of a certified medical condition . Applications for extension (whether they be medical or exceptional circumstances) shall be made by the due date and time for the assessment unless the exceptional circumstance or medical condition is such that the student cannot reasonably be expected to have applied prior to the due date.
other_assessment_information:
The Mid Semester Exam and Final Exam are Open Book exams. Students may bring any material (except on electronic devices) with them into the exam venues. These include Study Notes, tutorial questions and answers, case studies and the prescribed textbook. But, as there is limited time available to answer questions under exam conditions, it is not practical to look up all, many or most of the answers. It is therefore important that students have an effective plan to prepare for the Open Book exams. One approach would be to prepare a summary of the most important points from the legislation in alphabetical order and to expand and amend this summary as the semester progresses, noting that a pre-requisite of an effective summary is to know the examinable work in the first instance. Due to space constraints in exam venues, it is unlikely practical to place all of the material brought into exam venues on the desk space available.

Relying on the Open Book exam regime to find answers under exam conditions is not an effective strategy. There is no substitute for knowing the material.

As the capacity of tutorial rooms is limited, and due to Occupational Health and Safety Guidelines, you must attend the tutorial you are signed on to. A roll will be called if venues are over-filled which limits the time available to discuss tutorial answers.
